one_line_takeaway: A design-science governance framework (not an empirical evaluation) that argues AI-assisted SOC query generation should be brokered — AI plans, a policy-enforced broker with schema grounding, approved templates, and read-only adapters decides what actually executes — with maturity stages and ERB gates for promoting from proof-of-concept to production, while explicitly disclaiming any tested production-effectiveness evidence.
key_finding: There is no empirical finding to report because none of the proposed evaluation stages (offline benchmark, historical replay, purple-team simulation, shadow-mode pilot, limited production) were actually executed in this paper — it is a conceptual/framework contribution. The paper's substantive claim is a design argument: separating AI 'planning' from operational 'execution' via a broker (schema grounding + approved templates + policy validation + read-only adapters + audit trace + ERB gates) is proposed as more governable than either unbrokered AI query generation (flexible but uncontrolled) or a static approved-query library (governed but inflexible), and read-only access alone ('KQL is read-only') is argued to be necessary but not sufficient for safety because broad scans, sensitive-field exposure, cost overruns, and misleading interpretations remain possible even without write access.
core_contribution: A design science research (DSR) paper that proposes a management/governance framework — not a new technical system — for how engineering managers should govern AI-assisted operational decision support (LLM planning, RAG, coding agents) inside high-risk, privileged environments. The paper's own instantiation is a conceptual 'governed AI query-broker' architecture for Kusto Query Language (KQL)/Microsoft Azure security telemetry (Defender XDR, Sentinel, Sentinel data lake, Azure Data Explorer): AI models are restricted to producing a structured 'hunt plan' that must pass through RAG schema grounding, an approved-template resolver, a policy/RBAC/cost broker, and read-only source adapters before any query executes, with full request-to-disposition audit trails. The paper explicitly states its contribution is NOT a new KQL technique, detection algorithm, or security product, but rather six design propositions (brokered execution, template-first generation, provenance-aware threat intelligence, evidence-bounded evaluation, sandboxed agentic contribution, role-centered governance), a maturity model (Stage 0 offline review through Stage 4 agent-assisted engineering), Engineering Review Board (ERB) promotion gates, a risk register, and an evaluation *protocol* (not evaluation results) spanning offline benchmark, historical replay, purple-team simulation, shadow-mode pilot, and limited production stages.
threat_model: Not an attacker-vs-defender security threat model in the classic sense; the paper frames organizational/operational risk from AI-assisted read-only query generation in privileged SOC telemetry. Named risks: schema hallucination (AI references unavailable tables/fields), excessive data scans (missing time bounds/row caps causing cost or privacy exposure), sensitive-field exposure via broad projections, weak/stale threat-intelligence indicators driving false conclusions, prompt injection (telemetry content such as command lines, URLs, filenames, and email subjects being attacker-controlled text that could be misinterpreted as instructions rather than evidence), agentic overreach by coding agents (attempting to merge, deploy, or access secrets/production APIs), and analyst overreliance/unclear accountability. The paper is explicit that KQL is read-only in ordinary use and that response actions (device isolation, account disablement, email purge, detection-rule deployment) are explicitly out of scope and would require separate governance.
limitations: Limitations stated explicitly by the authors in Section 11: (1) the artifact is designed primarily for Microsoft-centric SOCs (Defender XDR/Sentinel/ADX); generalization to Splunk, Elastic, or other cloud-native SIEMs is unverified since adapters and schemas would differ. (2) The study specifies an evaluation protocol and evidence boundary but does not claim multi-enterprise production performance — no such evidence exists yet. (3) Synthetic/schema-normalized public datasets proposed for reproducibility cannot reproduce tenant-specific telemetry gaps, licensing differences, benign administrative noise, or real adversary behavior. (4) LLM and coding-agent outputs can vary across model versions, prompts, and tool manifests (reliability threat). (5) Role-centered governance depends on organizational maturity; the broker is not a substitute for foundational SOC capabilities in less-mature organizations. The authors also list their own threats-to-validity (construct, internal, external, reliability) in Section 10, acknowledging KQL validity/execution-success metrics do not capture analyst judgment or managerial value, and that external validity is bounded to Microsoft-centric ecosystems.

baselines_compared: Manual analyst-written KQL (proposed baseline for future empirical evaluation, representing expert human performance) — not executed in this paper
Static approved query library (proposed baseline representing governance without AI flexibility) — not executed in this paper
Unbrokered AI-generated KQL (proposed baseline representing flexibility with weak control) — not executed in this paper
Full brokered architecture (the paper's proposed artifact) — not executed/benchmarked in this paper
